summary:
The Islamic Conquest of Egypt (639–642 AD) was one of the most strategically significant campaigns of the [[event:2126|Rashidun Caliphate]], transforming the wealthiest province of the Byzantine Empire into an Islamic territory that would never return to Roman or Christian control. The conquest was led by Amr ibn al-As, who with a relatively small Arab force — reportedly around 4,000 warriors initially reinforced to perhaps 12,000 — defeated Byzantine defenders at the Battle of Heliopolis (640 AD) and besieged the fortress of Babylon near modern Cairo before capturing Alexandria in 642 AD.

The speed and completeness of the conquest reflected multiple factors beyond Arab military skill: Egypt's Coptic Christian population had deep theological grievances against Byzantine religious policy (the Council of Chalcedon controversy), making the population reluctant to fight for their Byzantine overlords; the devastating Byzantine-Persian wars of 603–628 AD had exhausted Egypt's defenses; and the plague of Justinian had reduced the population and Byzantine garrison strength. Amr ibn al-As proved a capable administrator as well as general, preserving much of the existing administrative structure and allowing Coptic Christians to practice their religion under dhimmi status. The conquest of Egypt gave the new Islamic state one of the most agriculturally productive regions in the known world, providing grain surpluses that helped fund further expansion across North Africa and into Spain. Egypt became and remains one of the most populous Muslim-majority countries in the world.

read_more_links.title: Amr ibn al-As Egyptian conquest general
